speaker: interviewer
text: Here we are. Actually, I'll do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is December the 12th. I'm sorry, it's December the 7th, 1995. I'm interviewing Hedy Epstein, born Wachemheimer. I'm Julie Heifetz. This is Saint Louis, Missouri, in the United States. And the interview will be in English. Hedy, could you tell us? Are you ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not quite yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The other thing is, Hedy, we-- we're going to get about 20% before the war, 60% during the war, and 20% at the end of the war. That's how the balance goes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Hedy, could you tell me about your family life before the war started-- when you were born and during your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I was born on August 15, 1924, in Freiburg, which is in southwest Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-R-E-I-B-- as in boy-- U-R-G. But I lived in a village named Kippenheim, K-I-P-P-E-N-H-E-I-M, which is about 40 kilometers north of Freiburg. And my family on my father's side of the family had lived in Kippenheim for many, many generations. My mother, or my mother's family, also had lived in Germany for many generations, but in another part of Germany. My mother came from Hanau, H-A-N-A-U, which is near Frankfurt, F-R-A-N-K-F-U-R-T. I was an only child. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was-- had a business, which he operated together with his younger brother, Oskar, O-S-K-A-R. It was a business that my great-grandfather started in 1857. And it was a dry good business. Over the years, the focus of the business changed. Initially, it was a store. But later on, I-- we also dealt with customers that were-- lived in other parts of Germany. And in fact, I know my father, once a year, made a business trip to Switzerland. So there must have been some businesses assoc-- association there. I did not know until I was six years old that I was Jewish. My family was a non-practicing family. We went to synagogue on the high holy days. But I had also been to church with our housekeeper. And I didn't really know the difference. It was just another place to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your housekeeper's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Paula, P-A-U-L-A. And when I entered first grade, at that time in Germany, religion was taught as part of the regular curriculum. And it was probably during the first week in school that the teacher said, the next hour is going to be religious instructions. And I don't know everyone's denominations, so would all the Catholic children please raise their hands? And up went my hand. And the teacher knew me, because she was a neighbor. And she said, Hedy, put your hand down. You're not Catholic. I said, all right. And then all the Catholic children were asked to go to room one, let's say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then she said, would all the Lutheran children raise their hands? And up went my hand again. And she said, Hedy, you're not Lutheran. Put your hand down, please. And then I began to question her. And I said, well, what am I? And she said, you're Jewish. And I said, no I'm not. I'm going to tell my mother about you. And my mother is going to straighten all this out. How would you have had a negative reaction to being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure about that. But all I can assume is that I heard something negative about being Jewish. What that was, or when that was, or who told me, I don't know that. And the teacher then insisted later on that I had to go with the other Jewish children to religious classes, wherever they were. And the whole hour, I protested that I don't have to listen. And I don't have to learn, because I'm not Jewish. And I'm going to tell my mother. And she's going to straighten all this out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I came home later that day. And I told my mother what happened. And my mother said, but you are Jewish. And I said, no I'm not. Why, are you? And she said, yes I am. And I said, is Daddy Jewish? Yes. Is grandma Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went through the whole family. And they all were Jewish, my mother said. And my final response was, well maybe all of you are. But I'm not. But I had go to the Jewish religion classes. What I really remember-- some things that I-- some highlights maybe about my family and how we lived-- my-- I always felt that we were outsiders, that we were not part of either the general community and also not part of the Jewish community. My parents were much more interested in the arts and literature. And that was very much apparent in the way we lived. There was no library in the village. But I remember my mother receiving boxes of books. And I don't know whether these were purchased books or whether they were library books. But I know I remember them coming. And I remember her reading a lot. One of the exciting things or highlights of my life were that every so often, the opera would be broadcast from the La Scala Opera House in Milan, Italy. And I was allowed to stay up, because it usually came on like about 9 o'clock at night or so, if I promised to take a nap earlier in the day. And I would follow, with my parents, the libretto. And I mean, to this day, I still know where certain arias are on the page. My father also frequently taught me proverbs in foreign languages usually. And one of them that I particularly remember, and I remember the setting-- we were sitting in the living room. And I was sitting on my father's lap. And I was about four years old. And my father taught me a Latin proverb, which really summarizes my life. But I didn't understand it at that time, or the philosophy of it. And the proverb is [LATIN SPEECH,] which, in translation, means not in school, but in life, you learn. There were many others that he taught me. But this one, for some reason, really stands out. And I used to write it on the flyleaf of all my books, probably much to the dismay of my teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember being told by one of the Jewish children that I'm not a good Jew. And I came home. And I asked, you know, what does it mean to be a good Jew? And why am I not a good Jew? And I remember my parents explaining to me that there's no such thing as a good Jew, or a bad Jew, or a good Christian, or a bad Christian. But there-- there's such a thing as a good human being that has certain moral and ethical values. And that's what I should adhere to and strive for.

speaker: interviewee
text: It bothered me that my parents were different, that I was different. And when I went to someone else's home, in some ways, I wanted to be-- I wanted my family to be the way that family was, the, the way those children were. But at the same time, I felt great discomfort.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways were they different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their value system seemed to be different. They were-- it's difficult for me to describe. But the-- I guess the, the value system that my par-- or the, the standard of life that my parents had-- the, you know, the, the music, the books, the art-- it wasn't there. And it bothered me that it wasn't there. And then I wanted to be back home. But when I was back home, I wished I could be-- or my parents could be the way the others were. And I've never really resolved that dilemma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the others had that your family seemed not to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They seemed to be more part of either the Jewish community, or the total community, or both. And we were the outsiders. And, you know, and even more than that, my moth-- the tradition was, I think, for marriages to be arranged. And they were amongst local people. And my mother was not from the local community. And it was not an arranged marriage. My parents had both attended a fair in Frankfurt, and both looking at the same exhibit at the same time, and started to talk to each other. And that is how they met. And so my mother was always the outsider, not only in the community, but also in my father's family. They never really totally accepted her. And I think it was, it was problematic for her. I mean, she was unhappy about that. And I picked that up and incorporated that into my being also. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I guess what I'd like to talk about is maybe the time that Hitler came to power, because that was the beginning of the change in my life, though I wasn't initially aware of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us-- before that, when you were a child growing up, what was your experience with anti-Semitism before this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Hitler? I don't think I was aware. Although, you know, as I mentioned earlier, I started school before Hitler came to power. And I denied that I was Jewish. So I must have picked up something somewhere. But what that was, I, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went to school with non-Jews as well Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't experience anything then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not experience any anti-Semitism in school. The first four years that I was in school-- and the first four years I went to the public school in the village where I lived, in, in Kippenheim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, I changed schools. And that's when I began to experience anti-Semitism in school. However, I was aware of anti-Semitism prior to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how were you aware of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first thing was when Hitler came to power on January 30, 1933, I remember my parents and other adults talking about Hitler and saying that they hoped his regime wouldn't last, that he was a bad person. And I didn't, at that time, really think it had very much to do with me. I just thought it was some adults talking a lot of nonsense. But just two months later, on Saturday, April 1, 1933, I realized that perhaps it did indeed have something to do with me. Because on that day, there was a boycott of Jewish businesses all over Germany. The purpose of the boycott was to prevent Christians from shopping in Jewish stores. And as I mentioned earlier, my father had a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I recall that there was a Nazi standing in front of my father's business. Another one was standing in front of the Jewish butcher, the Jewish bakery, the Jewish hardware store. And I asked my father, what is this all about? And he said, you know, don't worry. It's nothing. It'll go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And indeed, it did. That boycott lasted only that one day. However, there were long-range repercussions. Because after that, Christians were afraid to shop in Jewish stores. And so there were economic repercussions, which I sort of sensed in terms of that maybe be things were a little more difficult financially for my family, though it was never talked about. But I think I sort of picked it up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember on a building right across from where my father's business was, there was a glass case, which displayed one of the Nazi newspapers. It was called That's D-E-R, and then a new word, S-T-U-umlaut-R-M-E-R. And I-- my parents had told me to not go over there and read that paper. If I walk past that building, just walk past and don't look at it. And of course, I did look at it. And I remember seeing horrible, ugly caricatures of Jews with big, hooked noses, and being fat and hunchbacked, and being greedy. So I knew that that had something to do with Hitler. The next major event that really brought anti-Semitism to my attention, though I may not have been able to call it anti-Semitism at that time, was in 1935. My parents had decided that I should attend school in a neighboring community, because the educational level in that school was higher. It was called a Real Gymnasium. That's capital R-E-A-L G-Y-M-N-A-S-I-U-M. And I went with my father to be enrolled. And the principal said, I'm sorry, but Hedy can't come to school here, because you're Jewish. And my father, without saying a word, pointed to a pin that he always wore very proudly in the lapel of his jacket. And the principal said, I'm sorry. I didn't realize that you are a wounded veteran of World War I. In that case, Hedy can come to school here. And when I started in that school, there were several Jewish children in the-- in the school, a number of them from my own community. But over the next few years, the number of Jewish children decreased, to the point when I left that school several years later, there was only one other Jewish child and I in that school. And the reason for the decrease of the, of the number of Jewish children was because their families were fortunate enough to be able to leave Germany. It was at that time probably relatively easy to leave Germany, because the Nazis wanted to get rid of the Jews. It was more difficult to find a country that would receive you and someone in that country to sponsor you. My parents had also tried desperately to find relatives somewhere, somehow, who could sponsor us and so that we could leave. But we had not been successful. And as the number of Jewish children decreased in that school, the anti-Semitism seemed to increase. And as much as the Christian children no longer talked to me, or if they did talk to me, they'd call me a dirty Jew, or some other choice names. Recess was the loneliest time of the day, because no one would talk to me. And no one would play with me. And I also had the great misfortune the last two and a half years that I was in that school to have had a math teacher who was an SS man. The SS were the Nazi elite, the storm troopers. And he came to school almost daily, wearing his black SS uniform and knee-high black boots. And in his right boot, he always carried a revolver. And whenever he asked me a question, he wou-- and he asked me lots of questions-- he would either have his hand at the revolved-- on the revolver, or a couple times, he actually pointed it at me. And no matter what my answers were, whether they were right or wrong, he would ridicule me in front of my classmates and say, that's a Jewish answer. And we all know Jewish answers are no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember his name. And the interesting thing is in 1990, I was at the school. And I talked to the students and the faculty. And the principal of the school looked up his name and told me his name. And I, again, have forgotten it. I think there's a good reason why I've forgotten it and why I don't want to remember it. In, in fact, because of this math teacher, I have real problems, even today, still figuring out arithmetic, never mind math problems. Whether I do this manually or whether I use a computer, this SS man walks across the page or across the computer. I, I want to just digress for a minute, if I may. When I was in the school in 1990, I talked about this. And I told the students, you know, now I'm in the place where this happened. And maybe there's something that can be done so I can rid myself of this teacher. And I'm looking to you. Maybe you have an idea how this can be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no one said anything until I talked to the last group of students, which was the youngest group. And near the end, one of the students said, you asked us to help you. And we haven't done anything. And I said, well, do you have any ideas? And he said, yes. And he turned round, pointed at one of the other students and said, he's very good at drawing. Why don't you describe how the teacher looks? And he can draw him on the blackboard. And so this student came forward and drew him on the blackboard. And then I said to this student whose idea it was, but now, he's present even more than he's been before. Now what are we going to do? And this future psychologist, I think, said, why don't you pick up the wet sponge, stand back from the board-- the blackboard, and throw it at this picture? And see if you can erase him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did that. And as I was doing it, there were just all kinds of feelings that came out of me that I wasn't even aware of. And I wasn't totally capable of erasing him by this one toss of the sponge. And so I handed the sponge to the student whose idea it was, and he threw it. And then the others threw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the end, there was still a little bit left. And I said, now what are we going to do? And the same student suggested that I pick up the chalk and just scratch him out. And I just went, ah, ah, ah, and got rid of some more feelings. And lo and behold, I now can do a little more than I could before. Like for instance, I can now work percentages, which I was tot-- totally unable to do before. Interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: But back to the 30's, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the next major thing, I guess, that happened was in 1936. My grandfather, my mother's father, Heinrich Eichel H-E-I-N-R-I-C-H E-I-C-H-E-L, lived in Hanau, H-A-N-A-U. And one day, he was walking on the sidewalk when a Nazi came up to him and said, Jews aren't allowed to walk on the sidewalk. Get off. And so he walked in the street. And the same Nazi said, and Jews aren't allowed to walk on the side-- on the street, either. You're under arrest. And with that, he was taken to a jail. After spending several days in this awful jail, he was told, if you promise to give up your business, give up your home, and leave town, and never come back again, we will release you. And since he wanted so desperately to get out, he made that promise. But now, he no longer had a source of income. He no longer had a place to live. And so he came to live with us. And at first, I thought it was wonderful having Grandpa there every day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until I found out why and began to understand why he was so unhappy. He spent several months with us. And then when he felt that maybe it was too much for us, he moved on to my uncle and aunt's home, to his son and daughter-in-law, and spent several months with them. That was my uncle, Manfred, M-A-N-F-R-E-D, and my aunt, Else, E-L-S-E. And he spent several months with them. And then when he felt it was maybe too much for them, he moved on to an old age home, spent some time there. And then came back to us, and then onto my uncle and aunt, and so on. The next major thing that really happened would take me to November 9 and November 10, 1938, which is Crystal Night, or the Night of the Broken Glass. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The night of, of, of November 9, I remember my parents shutting the, the shutters on the windows, and my father saying to me, if you hear any noise during the night, get up out of bed immediately and go and sit in the wardrobe in the hallway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, why are you saying that? What do you mean? And my father, as well as my mother, who always answered all of my questions at any other time, my father said, don't ask any questions. Just do as you're told.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to bed. And it was a quiet night. Nothing happened. And the next morning, I really had forgotten about it and proceeded to get ready for school and left, as I always did. And I remember, it was a very cold, very sunny, bright day. And I-- on my way to school, I had to pass the home and office of a Jewish dentist. And all the windows in that building were broken. And though I didn't know why, I was quite sure it was because the man was Jewish. And as I entered the schoolyard, I could sense an air of excitement. But I was unable to identify it. And I was also afraid to ask. And so I just proceeded on to my classroom. Classes started punctuality at eight o'clock. At about 8:30, the principal walked in. And he talked to all of us. And at some point, he stopped. And he pointed his finger at me. And he said, get out you, dirty Jew. And I heard what he said, but I just could not believe that this man, who I thought was a really kind, gentle person-- his daughter was one of my classmates-- that he would say something like this. And so I--

speaker: interviewer
text: By this time, you were how old, Hedy?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14. And I asked him to repeat it. And he not only repeated it, but came over and took me by the elbow and shoved me out the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I was standing outside in the hallway, all kinds of thoughts raced through my head, like, you know, what did I do? Did I not pay attention? Did I yawn? What am I going to tell my parents? And I guess what I was-- the questions that I was asking, for which there were no answers really, is typical of what victims do. Rather than realizing that the problem is outside of themselves, I looked to myself. What have I done? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And before I could answer those questions, the students came running out, put on their coats. Some of them pushed or shoved me. Others called me dirty Jew and some other names. And they left. And I had no idea where they were going. And so I returned back to the classroom, sat at my desk, and got out a book, when there was a knock on the door. And in came the only other Jewish child in that school, a boy by the name of Hans Durlacher. That's H-A-N-S D-U-R-L-A-C-H-E-R. Hans was a year younger than I, and in a grade below me. And he had pretty much the same experience as I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're at a good stopping point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.
